turns.content: I'm trying to fix a failing CI/CD pipeline where our velocity calculations keep breaking because we're mixing m/s and km/h everywhere. It feels exactly like using the wrong type in my old Python bootcamp projects, but Rust should catch this at compile time somehow.

That's a classic case of missing dimensional analysis. The dimensioned crate is designed specifically for this so you can't accidentally add meters to seconds without a compiler error. It uses traits like Dimension and Quantity which enforce that your units actually match before the operation happens.
I've heard of dimensioned but I'm not sure how to integrate it into our existing Cargo.toml setup without breaking everything else in our codebase. Has anyone done this migration successfully?

You can add dimensioned as a dev-dependency first to test your calculations, then move it to regular dependencies once verified. The crate exports traits like Quantity and Dimension which you'll need to implement for your velocity types so the type system knows they are compatible.
So if I have a Velocity struct defined in our codebase, how exactly do I make it work with dimensioned's Quantity trait without rewriting all my logic?

You don't need to rewrite everything if you wrap your existing Velocity type. The dimensioned crate provides a macro or trait bound that lets you annotate your struct so it automatically gets the correct unit properties like magnitude and dimensions.
That sounds promising but I'm worried about runtime performance since we're running this in our CI pipeline every minute. Will dimensioned add any noticeable overhead?

The overhead is minimal because the actual math happens at compile time through trait implementations and generics. At runtime, it's just regular arithmetic on your numbers since the unit safety checks don't execute during execution.
